title: Real Analysis
author: H. L. Royden, Patrick M. Fitzpatrick
description: The definitive graduate-level textbook on measure theory, integration, and functional analysis. Royden's approach organises real analysis as a single coherent subject: measure in Part I, abstract topological and Banach/Hilbert space theory in Part II, and general measure and integration in Part III. Long the standard first-year graduate analysis text at top U.S. mathematics departments.
rationale: Selected as the root book for real-and-complex-analysis because it has served since 1963 as the single canonical graduate analysis text at institutions including Princeton, Stanford, and Rice. Its part-structure makes it uniquely suited as an organising spine for this subcategory; every subsequent analytic text in the subcategory (Folland, Rudin R&CA, Hewitt-Stromberg) either presupposes it or directly competes with it as a foundational reference.
excerpt: The definitive graduate-level treatment of measure theory, integration, and functional analysis that defined the standard curriculum for decades.
keyIdeas: Lebesgue measure and integration as the foundation of modern real analysis
Complete abstract treatment of metric, topological, Banach, and Hilbert spaces
Dual characterization of Banach spaces via Hahn-Banach, representing a deep structural theory
Integration over abstract measure spaces with Radon-Nikodym and product measure theorems
Convergence theorems (Vitali, Egoroff, Riesz-Fischer) as the operational toolkit of analysts
keyTakeaways: Measure theory subsumes and extends Riemann integration, enabling uniform integrability arguments
The Hahn-Banach theorem is the single most powerful tool for constructing linear functionals
Density of C_c in L^p and the Riesz-Markov theorem characterize dual spaces concretely
Stone-Weierstrass and Tietze extension theorems reconcile topology with functional analysis
Ergodic theory emerges naturally from invariant measure theory (Bogoliubov-Krilov)
whoShouldRead: First-year graduate students in mathematics beginning real analysis sequences
Analysts and applied mathematicians needing authoritative measure theory reference
Physicists and engineers working with Lebesgue integration in functional settings
whoShouldSkip: Students seeking an intuition-first introduction to analysis (Rudin or Apostol are gentler)
Readers who need extensive worked examples — Royden is famously terse
